content.data:
Boa noite! Que história legal — um jogo de terror em labirinto no Roblox, isso tem potencial!

Sobre compartilhar o projeto comigo: infelizmente não consigo acessar o Roblox Studio diretamente nem me conectar à sua conta. Mas existem algumas formas de eu te ajudar mesmo assim:

**O que você pode me enviar:**

1. **Screenshots do cenário** — você abre o projeto no Roblox Studio e tira prints do labirinto, dos NPCs, do que já foi feito. Posso analisar imagens.

2. **Os scripts Lua existentes** — no Roblox Studio, você pode abrir o Explorer, clicar nos scripts e copiar o código. Cola aqui e eu leio tudo.

3. **Descrição do que já existe** — se não quiser abrir agora, me conta de memória: como é o labirinto, o que os NPCs fazem (ou deveriam fazer), qual era a ideia do jogo.

**O que eu consigo fazer por você:**

- Escrever os scripts Lua do zero (ou corrigir os antigos)
- Explicar como configurar cada coisa no Studio passo a passo
- Planejar toda a mecânica do jogo (IA dos monstros, sistema de susto, progressão)
- Te guiar mesmo sendo leigo — vou explicar onde clicar, o que fazer

Roblox usa **Lua** como linguagem de script, que é bem simples. Com o que você me descrever, a gente monta um plano e eu escrevo o código — você só precisa colocar no lugar certo no Studio.

Por onde quer começar? Me conta o que você lembra do projeto — a ideia do jogo, o que os NPCs deveriam fazer, qual era o objetivo do jogador no labirinto.

Haha entendi a referência ao CDK! No Roblox não tem exatamente isso, mas tem algumas opções:

**Exportar o projeto:**
- O arquivo do jogo é salvo como `.rbxl` (Roblox place file) — você pode me enviar esse arquivo diretamente. Eu consigo ler o XML dentro dele e ver a estrutura, scripts, objetos, tudo.
- No Studio: **File → Save to File** → gera o `.rbxl`

Esse arquivo é basicamente tudo do seu projeto. Se você me mandar, consigo ver o que já existe.

---

**Sobre melhorias e monetização — vou ser direto:**

A base que você descreveu (labirinto + monstros + arma) é sólida. O que faz um jogo de terror no Roblox bombar hoje:

**Melhorias de gameplay:**
- Sistema de stamina (correr esgota, cria tensão)
- Monstros com IA real — patrulha, perseguição, campo de visão
- Esconderijos funcionais (armários, embaixo de mesas)
- Geração procedural do labirinto — cada run é diferente, aumenta replay
- Sistema de sanidade — ficar perto do monstro distorce a tela
- Múltiplos monstros com comportamentos diferentes

**Retenção de jogadores:**
- Modo cooperativo (2-4 jogadores) — jogos co-op de terror viralizam muito
- Sistema de progressão — desbloqueio de skins, armas, dificuldades
- Recordes e leaderboard
- Dificuldades (fácil/médio/difícil/impossível)

**Monetização (Robux):**
- **Game Passes** — acesso a modo difícil, personagem exclusivo, vida extra
- **Developer Products** — continuar após morte sem reiniciar
- **Skins de personagem/arma** — puramente cosmético, não pay-to-win
- **VIP Server** — jogadores pagam pra ter servidor privado com amigos

O modelo mais lucrativo no Roblox hoje é cosmético + VIP server. Pay-to-win afasta jogadores.

---

Me manda o `.rbxl` se conseguir achar, aí a gente vê o que já tem e planeja o que falta construir.

Qual conta do GitHub usar?

1. **pessoal** (MatheusRibeir098)
2. **dati** (matheuspribeiro-dati)

O `gh` foi instalado mas não tem nenhuma conta autenticada ainda. Preciso que você faça o login. Execute o comando abaixo no seu terminal:

```bash
gh auth login
```

Quando rodar, ele vai perguntar:
1. **GitHub.com** → Enter
2. **HTTPS** → Enter
3. **Login with a web browser** → Enter
4. Vai aparecer um código — você abre o link no navegador, cola o código e autoriza

Depois que fizer o login, me avisa que eu faço o push e finalizo a tarefa! 🚀

Tudo pronto! ✅

**Repositório criado:** https://github.com/MatheusRibeir098/roblox-labirinto-terror

---

Agora, como pegar o `.rbxl` do seu projeto antigo:

1. Abra o **Roblox Studio**
2. Abra o seu projeto do labirinto
3. No menu superior: **File → Save to File As...**
4. Salva como `projeto.rbxl` em qualquer pasta fácil de achar (ex: Desktop)
5. Me manda o arquivo aqui no chat

Se o projeto estiver salvo na nuvem do Roblox (Roblox.com), vai aparecer na tela inicial do Studio em **My Games** — é só abrir de lá e salvar o arquivo.

Perfeito! Vejo o menu Arquivo aberto. O que você precisa é:

**"Baixar uma cópia"**

Clica nessa opção — ela vai salvar o arquivo `.rbxl` no seu computador. Salva na área de trabalho (Desktop) pra ficar fácil de achar.

Depois é só me mandar o arquivo aqui no chat!

Tudo no GitHub! ✅

---

Já consegui ver o que tem no seu projeto. Aqui o resumo do que identifiquei:

**O que já existe:**
- 🔫 **Pistola** — arma do jogador (com sistema de munição `AmmoCapacity`)
- 💀 **ColdBloodedKiller** — um NPC/monstro (que nome assustador!)
- 💬 **Sistema de diálogo** — `NpcChat`, `DialogoGui`, `DialogoEvent`
- 🖥️ **Tela Inicial** — menu já existe
- 🎮 **Sistema de armas** — `WeaponsSystemFolder`, `WeaponsSystemGui`
- 🔦 **Iluminação** — `PointLight`, `SpotLight`, `Night Sky`
- ⚡ **Interações** — `HazardSwitch`, `FireButton`, `ProximityPrompt`
- 🏠 **Lobby** — `LobbyCC`

Já tem uma base bem sólida! O próximo passo seria você abrir o projeto no Studio e me mandar screenshots do cenário para eu ver como está visualmente. Aí a gente planeja o que implementar primeiro — provavelmente a IA do `ColdBloodedKiller` e o sistema de fuga/esconderijo.

Ótimo, pesquisa completa. Agora vou te explicar tudo de forma clara:

---

## Como funciona um projeto Roblox profissional

### 1. Colaboração em tempo real — Team Create

O Roblox tem uma feature nativa chamada **Team Create**. É o jeito mais simples de trabalhar com seu amigo:

- Você ativa no Studio: **View → Team Create → Enable**
- Convida o usuário Roblox do seu amigo
- Os dois abrem o projeto ao mesmo tempo e editam em tempo real, como Google Docs

**Limitação:** os dois precisam estar online ao mesmo tempo. E se dois mexerem no mesmo script, pode dar conflito.

---

### 2. Workflow profissional com Git — usando Azul

Para trabalhar de forma assíncrona (cada um no seu tempo) e com Git, o fluxo moderno em 2026 é:

```
Roblox Studio ←→ Azul (plugin) ←→ arquivos .lua no PC ←→ Git/GitHub
```

**Como funciona:**
- **Azul** é um plugin do Studio que sincroniza os scripts do jogo com arquivos `.lua` no seu PC em tempo real
- Você edita o script no VS Code → salva → aparece no Studio automaticamente
- Você mexe no Studio → o arquivo `.lua` no PC atualiza
- Aí você faz `git commit` e `git push` normalmente
- Seu amigo faz `git pull` e tem os scripts atualizados

**O que vai para o Git:** só os scripts `.lua`. O cenário (partes, modelos, texturas) fica salvo na nuvem do Roblox.

---

### 3. Como eu (Kiro) posso te ajudar diretamente

Aqui está o que consigo fazer de verdade:

| O que eu faço | Como funciona |
|---|---|
| ✅ **Escrever scripts Lua completos** | Você cola no Studio |
| ✅ **Corrigir bugs nos scripts** | Você me manda o código, eu corrijo |
| ✅ **Planejar toda a arquitetura** | IA dos monstros, sistemas, eventos |
| ✅ **Guardar scripts no GitHub** | Com Azul, os `.lua` ficam no repositório |
| ✅ **Versionar o projeto** | Histórico de tudo que foi feito |
| ❌ **Acessar o Studio diretamente** | Não existe integração direta |
| ❌ **Ver o cenário visualmente** | Só via screenshots que você me manda |

---

### Recomendação para vocês dois

Dado que são iniciantes, sugiro começar simples:

**Fase 1 — agora:** usar **Team Create** para colaborar. Zero configuração, funciona na hora.

**Fase 2 — quando quiserem profissionalizar:** instalar **Azul + VS Code** para ter os scripts no GitHub e eu poder escrever/corrigir código diretamente nos arquivos.

---

Quer que eu monte o passo a passo para ativar o Team Create e convidar seu amigo? É bem rápido.

Exatamente! O fluxo seria esse:

```
Eu escrevo o .lua → git push → você faz git pull → Azul sincroniza → aparece no Studio
```

Mas tem um detalhe importante: **não é 100% automático**. Você precisaria rodar um comando no PC para puxar as atualizações. O fluxo completo seria:

1. Eu escrevo/corrijo o script aqui
2. Faço `git push` para o GitHub
3. Você abre o terminal e roda `git pull`
4. O Azul detecta a mudança e atualiza o Studio automaticamente

Então você não precisaria nem abrir o código — só rodar `git pull` e ver a mudança aparecer no Studio.

---

**Quer configurar isso agora?**

O que você precisaria instalar no Windows:
- **Node.js** (para rodar o Azul)
- **Azul** (via terminal: `npm install -g azul-sync`)
- **Plugin do Azul** no Roblox Studio (marketplace gratuito)

Leva uns 10 minutos. Quer que eu te guie passo a passo?

```
Instale o ambiente de desenvolvimento Roblox no Windows seguindo estes passos:

1. Instale o Node.js LTS: https://nodejs.org — marque "Add to PATH" durante a instalação

2. Abra o PowerShell como administrador e rode:
   npm install -g azul-sync

3. Instale o plugin do Azul no Roblox Studio:
   https://create.roblox.com/store/asset/79510309341601/Azul-Easy-Powerful-Studiofirst-Sync

4. Instale o VS Code: https://code.visualstudio.com

5. Instale o Git para Windows: https://git-scm.com/download/win
   Durante a instalação, escolha "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"

6. Configure o Git com a conta pessoal:
   git config --global user.name "MatheusRibeir098"
   git config --global user.email "matheusprfh098@gmail.com"

7. Clone o repositório do projeto:
   git clone https://github.com/MatheusRibeir098/roblox-labirinto-terror.git
   cd roblox-labirinto-terror

8. Abra a pasta no VS Code:
   code .

Confirme quando tudo estiver instalado.
```

```
/chat save ~/forge/projects/roblox-labirinto-terror/chat-roblox.json
```

Esse comando salva o histórico completo dessa conversa. Para retomar depois é só rodar:

```
/chat load ~/forge/projects/roblox-labirinto-terror/chat-roblox.json
```

# 🎃 Labirinto do Terror — Roblox

Jogo de terror no Roblox onde o jogador deve atravessar um labirinto infestado de monstros.

## Conceito

O jogador começa com uma arma e precisa chegar ao outro lado do labirinto.
No caminho, encontra monstros que pode **fugir**, **se esconder** ou **tentar matar**.

## Mecânicas planejadas

- [ ] Sistema de stamina (correr esgota energia)
- [ ] IA de monstros (patrulha, perseguição, campo de visão)
- [ ] Esconderijos funcionais (armários, embaixo de mesas)
- [ ] Geração procedural do labirinto
- [ ] Sistema de sanidade (distorção de tela perto de monstros)
- [ ] Modo cooperativo (2-4 jogadores)
- [ ] Sistema de progressão (skins, armas, dificuldades)
- [ ] Leaderboard

## Monetização

- **Game Passes** — modo difícil, personagem exclusivo, vida extra
- **Developer Products** — continuar após morte
- **Skins** — cosméticos de personagem e arma
- **VIP Server** — servidor privado com amigos

## Estrutura do repositório

```
/docs          → design, mecânicas, referências
/scripts       → scripts Lua do jogo
/assets        → referências visuais, concept art
projeto.rbxl   → arquivo do Roblox Studio (adicionar depois)
```

## Status

🚧 Em desenvolvimento — resgatando projeto antigo e expandindo.

# Game Design Document — Labirinto do Terror

## Visão Geral

Jogo de terror single/multiplayer no Roblox. O jogador atravessa um labirinto
enfrentando monstros com três abordagens: fuga, furtividade ou combate.

## Loop de Gameplay

1. Jogador spawna na entrada do labirinto com uma arma
2. Navega pelo labirinto em busca da saída
3. Encontra monstros ao longo do caminho
4. Chega à saída → vitória / morre → tela de game over

## Monstros (planejado)

| Monstro | Comportamento | Fraqueza |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| Perseguidor | Corre atrás do jogador ao ver | Pode ser morto com tiros |
| Patrulheiro | Rota fixa, campo de visão limitado | Pode ser evitado |
| Emboscador | Fica parado, ataca ao aproximar | Difícil de matar |

## Sistemas

### Stamina
- Correr consome stamina
- Stamina regenera ao andar ou parar
- Sem stamina = velocidade reduzida

### Sanidade
- Diminui ao ficar perto de monstros
- Efeitos visuais progressivos (tremor, distorção, escuridão)
- Chega a zero = game over

### Esconderijos
- Armários, caixas, embaixo de mesas
- Monstro perde o rastro se jogador se esconder a tempo

## Dificuldades

- **Fácil** — monstros lentos, mais munição, labirinto menor
- **Médio** — padrão
- **Difícil** — monstros rápidos, pouca munição, labirinto maior
- **Impossível** — Game Pass exclusivo

## Referências

- Doors (Roblox) — sistema de susto e progressão por salas
- Flee the Facility (Roblox) — fuga e esconderijo
- Outlast — sanidade e câmera

- Armários, caixas, embaixo de mesas
- Monstro perde o rastro se jogador se esconder a tempo

## Dificuldades

- **Fácil** — monstros lentos, mais munição, labirinto menor
- **Médio** — padrão
- **Difícil** — monstros rápidos, pouca munição, labirinto maior
- **Impossível** — Game Pass exclusivo

## Referências

- Doors (Roblox) — sistema de susto e progressão por salas
- Flee the Facility (Roblox) — fuga e esconderijo
- Outlast — sanidade e câmera

input.command:
python3 -c "
# O formato .rbxl binário do Roblox usa chunks com LZ4
# Vamos tentar extrair os chunks e descomprimir

import struct, sys

with open('/mnt/c/Users/Matheus/Documents/roblox-labirinto-terror/jogo.rbxl', 'rb') as f:
    data = f.read()

# Header: '<roblox!' (8 bytes) + signature (6 bytes) + num_classes (4) + num_instances (4) + reserved (8)
magic = data[:8]
print('Magic:', magic)

offset = 8
# Ler header do formato binário Roblox
# Signature bytes
sig = data[8:14]
print('Sig:', sig.hex())

# num_classes, num_instances
num_classes = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, 14)[0]
num_instances = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, 18)[0]
reserved = data[22:30]
print(f'Classes: {num_classes}, Instances: {num_instances}')

offset = 32  # após header de 32 bytes

# Ler chunks
chunks_info = []
while offset < len(data) - 16:
    chunk_name = data[offset:offset+4]
    if not all(32 <= b <= 126 for b in chunk_name):
        offset += 1
        continue
    
    compressed_size = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, offset+4)[0]
    uncompressed_size = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, offset+8)[0]
    reserved2 = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, offset+12)[0]
    
    name_str = chunk_name.decode('ascii', errors='ignore')
    chunks_info.append((name_str, compressed_size, uncompressed_size, offset))
    
    if compressed_size > 0 and compressed_size < len(data) - offset:
        offset += 16 + compressed_size
    else:
        offset += 16
    
    if len(chunks_info) > 50:
        break

print('Chunks encontrados:')
for c in chunks_info[:20]:
    print(f'  {c[0]}: compressed={c[1]}, uncompressed={c[2]}')
"

python3 << 'EOF'
import re, struct

with open('/mnt/c/Users/Matheus/Documents/roblox-labirinto-terror/jogo.rbxl', 'rb') as f:
    data = f.read()

results = []
i = 0
while i < len(data) - 5:
    size = struct.unpack_from('<I', data, i)[0]
    if 3 <= size <= 80 and i + 4 + size <= len(data):
        s = data[i+4:i+4+size]
        if all(32 <= b <= 126 for b in s):
            text = s.decode('ascii')
            if re.match(r'^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_ \-\.]{2,}$', text):
                results.append(text)
    i += 1

unique = list(dict.fromkeys(results))

# Nomes que claramente são do jogo (não do engine)
game_specific = [s for s in unique if any(keyword in s.lower() for keyword in [
    'npc', 'monster', 'monstro', 'labirinto', 'maze', 'terror', 'horror',
    'weapon', 'gun', 'pistol', 'pistola', 'arma', 'ammo', 'bullet',
    'player', 'spawn', 'door', 'porta', 'key', 'chave', 'exit', 'saida',

    re.match(r'^[A-Z][a-z]', s) and  # CamelCase

content.data:
Já consigo ver bastante coisa do seu projeto! Os destaques são:

- **`Pistola`** — a arma que o jogador pega
- **`ColdBloodedKiller`** — parece ser um NPC/monstro!
- **`NpcChat`** e **`DialogoGui`** e **`DialogoEvent`** — sistema de diálogo com NPCs
- **`Tela Inicial`** — tela de menu
- **`WeaponsSystemFolder`**, **`WeaponsSystemGui`**, **`WeaponsSystemWeapon`** — sistema de armas
- **`HazardSwitch`** e **`FireButton`** — interações no cenário
- **`LobbyCC`** — área de lobby

Agora vou copiar o arquivo para dentro do projeto e commitar:

Tudo no GitHub! ✅

---

Já consegui ver o que tem no seu projeto. Aqui o resumo do que identifiquei:

**O que já existe:**
- 🔫 **Pistola** — arma do jogador (com sistema de munição `AmmoCapacity`)
- 💀 **ColdBloodedKiller** — um NPC/monstro (que nome assustador!)
- 💬 **Sistema de diálogo** — `NpcChat`, `DialogoGui`, `DialogoEvent`
- 🖥️ **Tela Inicial** — menu já existe
- 🎮 **Sistema de armas** — `WeaponsSystemFolder`, `WeaponsSystemGui`
- 🔦 **Iluminação** — `PointLight`, `SpotLight`, `Night Sky`
- ⚡ **Interações** — `HazardSwitch`, `FireButton`, `ProximityPrompt`
- 🏠 **Lobby** — `LobbyCC`

Já tem uma base bem sólida! O próximo passo seria você abrir o projeto no Studio e me mandar screenshots do cenário para eu ver como está visualmente. Aí a gente planeja o que implementar primeiro — provavelmente a IA do `ColdBloodedKiller` e o sistema de fuga/esconderijo.

Legal saber que tem bastante coisa! Agora uma Pergunta, como os desenvolvedores trabalham junto num mesmo projeto? A gente vai precisar do repositorio do git? Pode ir salvado coisas lá depois importar para o projeto no roblox estudio?

Faça uma pesquisa preofunda na internet de todas as maneira que vc pode me ajudar diretamente!
Se não existe uma maneira de eu der acesso para vc e vc mexer diretamente no Projeto em Produção
E como compartilho com meu amigo para ele mexer junto!

Entenda, nunca fizemos isso antes, como deve funcionar um projeto desse no roblox
Boa pergunta! Deixa eu pesquisar como isso funciona de verdade no Roblox hoje em 2026:

content.data:
[ Developer Forum | Roblox ][1]

# [External development without Rojo: A guide to Azul, Rokit, Wally & Git][2]

[ Resources ][3] [ Community Tutorials ][4]
[scripting][5], [git][6], [github][7], [vscode][8]
[Ransomwavee][9] (Ransomwave) May 4, 2026, 3:14am 1

Welcome to the most comprehensive guide to syncing and external tooling in Roblox Studio! I’ll walk you through everything you need to know about external development, from setting up your environment to managing packages and version control. This guide is aimed towards *new* developers who want to move beyond the built-in editor and *existing* developers frustrated with traditional tools such as Rojo. In this guide, we’ll be covering:
* Visual Studio Code as your external IDE
* Rokit as your Toolchain Manager
* Wally as your Package Manager
* Version control w/ Git. I hope you find this useful!

# Introduction

## What is Syncing?

Syncing, in the context of Roblox, is the process of taking Scripts written on Roblox & displaying them in your PC and vice versa:
* A change in Studio → Updates a file on your PC
* A change in your PC → Updates a `Script` in Studio

This allows you to work with external tooling much easily, since Scripts are now Files on your local PC!

## What is Azul?

[Azul][10] is the tool we’ll be using to do the syncing process. Why Azul?

Rojo is industry-standard but requires a “Filesystem-first” workflow that can be tedious to maintain. Azul is “Studio-first,” meaning you can keep building in Studio like normal while Azul handles the heavy lifting in the background. You can read more about the differences on the [GitHub repo][11]. # Getting Started

## What you’ll need

You’ll need two main pieces of software:
* **[Visual Studio Code][12]:** The gold standard for code editors. * **[Node. js (NPM)][13]:** This is the engine that lets us run Azul on your computer. ## Installing Azul

We install Azul via your terminal (`Command Prompt` on Windows, `Terminal` on Mac). Run this command:

`npm install -g azul-sync
`

*(The `-g` flag is important, it installs it **globally** so you can use it in any folder!)*

## Installing the Azul Plugin

You need the “receiver” inside Roblox Studio

Grab it here: [Azul Plugin (Roblox Marketplace)][14]

## The Workflow

### Initial Setup
1. **Create a folder** on your PC (e. g. , `Desktop/MyRobloxGame`). 2. **Open VS Code**, go to `File > Open Folder`, and select that folder. 3. **Open a Terminal** inside VS Code (`Ctrl + ~` or `Terminal > New Terminal`). ### Actually Syncing
1. In your VS Code terminal, type:

`azul
`
2. Open your place in Roblox Studio. 3. Open the Azul Plugin and click **Connect**. And boom! Your scripts will start appearing in your file explorer. Edit a script in VS Code, hit `Ctrl + S`, and watch it update in Studio instantly. ## If all you needed was an easy way to sync your project, your journey can end here!

This guide will continue getting into **toolchain managers**, **package management** & **version control**. You may want to check these out if you’re interested in exploring external tooling!

# Toolchain Managers

## What are they?

Toolchain Managers are programs that automate the installation of development tools. For example, imagine a team project where many different tools are used. Without Toolchain Managers, you’d have to ask your teammates

> “What tools does this project use? Which versions of those tools?”

After which, you’d have to go and hunt for the correct tool and version for every project. It quickly becomes a mess once you work on multiple projects that use different toolsets & versions. With Toolchain Managers, this tedious task is automated. By running one command, the tool itself goes fetching for the correct tools and versions for you. ## What is Rokit?

[Rokit][15] is the most popular toolchain manager for Roblox projects. It exists as a direct successor to [Aftman][16], (which itself is a successor to [Foreman][17]). While many projects still use Aftman, [it’s a deprecated tool][18] and it’s generally discouraged to use it in new projects. ## Installing Rokit

Installing Rokit is very easy and requires 1-2 steps. Windows

Grab the latest release from the Rokit [Releases Page][19]. Run the executable you get after unzipping!

MacOS & Linux

Run the following in your terminal:

`curl -sSf https://raw. githubusercontent. com/rojo-rbx/rokit/main/scripts/install. sh | bash
`

Finally, initialize Rokit in your project by running:

`rokit init
`

These steps are directly taken from [the Rokit Repository][20]. # Package Managers

## What are they?

A package manager is a tool that helps you manage libraries and dependencies in your projects. It lets you easily install, update, and remove “Packages” (collections of code, i. e. libraries) that you can use in your Roblox games. ## What is Wally?

[Wally][21] is a package manager for Roblox, similar to `npm` *(which we used earlier!)*. It allows you to easily add third-party libraries to your projects, manage dependencies, and keep everything organized. For example, let’s say you want to use a popular library like react-lua. With Wally, you can simply run a command to install it, and Wally will handle the rest!

## Installing Wally

Installing Wally is very easy!

In your Project folder, run the following command:

`rokit add wally
`

Now, the `wally` command will be available!

## Using Wally

### First steps

Run the following command:

`wally init
`

This command creates Wally’s configuration files for you. Locate the previously mentioned `wally. toml` file
3. Under the `[dependencies]` section, add the following line:
   * `Promise = "evaera/roblox-lua-promise@1. 0. 0"
     `
4. Save the file and run the following command in your terminal:
   * `wally install
     `

This command tells Wally to read the `wally. toml` file, find the new dependency you added, and install it for you. After running this command, you will see Wally has created a new `Packages` folder in your project directory. Inside this folder, you’ll find a folder for the `Promise` package, which contains all the code for that library. 5. To get your packages inside Roblox Studio, run the following command:
   * `azul push --source Packages --destination ReplicatedStorage. Packages --rojo
     `
     * `azul push`: This command is used to “push” specific files & folders *(like this package!)* from your filesystem into Studio. * `--source`: Where the packages are located on your PC. * `--destination`: Where you want the packages to be in Roblox Studio. * `--rojo`: Since Wally packages are structured as Rojo projects, use this flag to tell Azul to treat the source folder as a Rojo project. * *(In place of `--source` or `--destination`, you can also write `-s` and `-d`!)*

> ### [:warning:] Important
> 
> You must run the `push` command every time you install a new package or update an existing one. > 
> To make this process easier, you can use Azul [per-place Daemon configurations][25], which let you run `azul push` with no arguments. > 
> To get started, find the “Open Daemon Settings” button under the Azul Plugin > Plugin Settings. This will create an Azul configuration specific to this game. > 
> Per-place configuration: Example
> 
> `-- The Azul Daemon will read this Module on connecting. > -- Push Mappings are how we tell Azul "what to push" (source) and "where to push it" (destination), 
> -- alongside settings like `--rojo` and `--destructive`. > return {
>   pushMappings = {
>       {
>           source = ". /Packages",
>           destination = { "ReplicatedStorage", "Packages" },
>           destructive = true,
>           rojoMode = true,
>       },
>   }
> }
> `

A few extra notes
* You can also install packages from the `[server-dependencies]` realm. Just add them under the `[server-dependencies]` section in `wally. toml` and push them to `ServerScriptService` instead of `ReplicatedStorage`. * In some specific cases, you may notice type errors when using Wally packages. That is because Wally thunks don’t export types. This is a known issue with Wally and can be fixed by running a 3rd-party tool called [`wally-package-types`][26]. # Version Control

## What is Git?

[Git][27] is the most popular version control system in the world. It allows you to keep track of changes to your code, collaborate with others, and manage different versions of your project. ## Why do I need it?

Version control is essential for any project, big or small. It allows you to:
* Keep a history of changes: You can see who made what changes and when. * Easily revert changes: If something breaks, you can easily go back to a previous version of your code. * Collaborate with others: Multiple people can work on the same project without overwriting each other’s work. * Experiment safely: You can create branches to try out new features without affecting the main codebase. While Roblox provides some basic version control features, they are not as powerful or flexible as Git, which is why many developers choose to use Git for their Roblox projects. ## Setting up Git

Visual Studio Code has built-in support for Git, making it super-duper easy to set up and use. Here’s how you can get started:
1. Navigate to the Source Control tab in VS Code (the icon that looks like a branch). 2. Initialize a new Git repository by clicking on the “Initialize Repository” button. * You’ll notice your files now appear green with a “U” next to them, which stands for “Untracked”. This means Git knows about these files, but they haven’t been committed yet!
3. Create a `. gitignore` file in the root of your project to tell Git which files or folders to ignore. * For Roblox projects, you typically want to ignore the `Packages` folder (since it can be reinstalled with Wally) and any local configuration files (like `. vscode/settings. json` or the `wally. lock` file). 4. Stage your changes by clicking the “+” icon next to the files you want to include in your commit. This tells Git that you want to include these changes in your next commit. 5. Write a commit message describing your changes and click “Commit” to save your changes to the local repository. And that’s it! You’ve successfully set up Git for your Roblox project. From here, you can start making commits & creating branches. If you want to distribute your code and collaborate with others, you can also push your local repository to a remote hosting service like GitHub. To do so:
6. Click on the “Publish Branch” button in the Source Control tab. 7. After following the prompts, your code will be pushed to a new repository on GitHub, and you can share the link with your collaborators!
   * For example, take a look at [BetterChassis][28]. A project I published to GitHub using Azul!

### Pack it up!

When publishing your repository, you may want to include more than just your source code. You may want to include assets, UI, or other Roblox instances in order for other developers to easily import your project in the state you left it at. To do this, you can use Azul’s “pack” command, which takes the current state of your game and encodes it into the `sourcemap. json` file. 1. Make sure your game is open in Roblox Studio. 2. Run the following command in your terminal:
   * `azul pack
     `

> ### [:warning:]
> 
> Remember that you’ll need to run this command **every time** you want to “save” the state of your game inside the `sourcemap. json`. It doesn’t update on its own!

If you want to run someone else’s project, you can use 2 commands:
* `azul build --from-sourcemap`:
  * If the project is a full game. * `azul push --from-sourcemap`:
  * If this project is meant to be imported into the game (like a library, a package, or a model). # Wrapping up

And that’s a wrap! You should now have a solid understanding of syncing in Roblox Studio, as well as how to use external tools like Rokit, Wally, and Git to manage your projects more efficiently. If you have any questions, or want any clarifications, be sure to ask below. This is the guide I wish I had back when I first started using external tools for Roblox development, and I hope it helps you on your journey as well!

# [External development without Rojo: A guide to Azul, Rokit, Wally & Git][2]

[ Resources ][3] [ Community Tutorials ][4]
[scripting][5], [git][6], [github][7], [vscode][8]
[Ransomwavee][9] (Ransomwave) May 4, 2026, 3:14am 1

Welcome to the most comprehensive guide to syncing and external tooling in Roblox Studio! I’ll walk you through everything you need to know about external development, from setting up your environment to managing packages and version control. This guide is aimed towards *new* developers who want to move beyond the built-in editor and *existing* developers frustrated with traditional tools such as Rojo. In this guide, we’ll be covering:
* Visual Studio Code as your external IDE
* Rokit as your Toolchain Manager
* Wally as your Package Manager
* Version control w/ Git. I hope you find this useful!

# Introduction

## What is Syncing?

Syncing, in the context of Roblox, is the process of taking Scripts written on Roblox & displaying them in your PC and vice versa:
* A change in Studio → Updates a file on your PC
* A change in your PC → Updates a `Script` in Studio

This allows you to work with external tooling much easily, since Scripts are now Files on your local PC!

## What is Azul?

[Azul][10] is the tool we’ll be using to do the syncing process. Why Azul?

Rojo is industry-standard but requires a “Filesystem-first” workflow that can be tedious to maintain. Azul is “Studio-first,” meaning you can keep building in Studio like normal while Azul handles the heavy lifting in the background. You can read more about the differences on the [GitHub repo][11]. # Getting Started

## What you’ll need

You’ll need two main pieces of software:
* **[Visual Studio Code][12]:** The gold standard for code editors. * **[Node. js (NPM)][13]:** This is the engine that lets us run Azul on your computer. ## Installing Azul

We install Azul via your terminal (`Command Prompt` on Windows, `Terminal` on Mac). Run this command:

`npm install -g azul-sync
`

*(The `-g` flag is important, it installs it **globally** so you can use it in any folder!)*

## Installing the Azul Plugin

You need the “receiver” inside Roblox Studio

Grab it here: [Azul Plugin (Roblox Marketplace)][14]

## The Workflow

### Initial Setup
1. **Create a folder** on your PC (e. g. , `Desktop/MyRobloxGame`). 2. **Open VS Code**, go to `File > Open Folder`, and select that folder. 3. **Open a Terminal** inside VS Code (`Ctrl + ~` or `Terminal > New Terminal`). ### Actually Syncing
1. In your VS Code terminal, type:

`azul
`
2. Open your place in Roblox Studio. 3. Open the Azul Plugin and click **Connect**. And boom! Your scripts will start appearing in your file explorer. Edit a script in VS Code, hit `Ctrl + S`, and watch it update in Studio instantly. ## If all you needed was an easy way to sync your project, your journey can end here!

This guide will continue getting into **toolchain managers**, **package management** & **version control**. You may want to check these out if you’re interested in exploring external tooling!

# Toolchain Managers

## What are they?

Toolchain Managers are programs that automate the installation of development tools. For example, imagine a team project where many different tools are used. Without Toolchain Managers, you’d have to ask your teammates

> “What tools does this project use? Which versions of those tools?”

After which, you’d have to go and hunt for the correct tool and version for every project. It quickly becomes a mess once you work on multiple projects that use different toolsets & versions. With Toolchain Managers, this tedious task is automated. By running one command, the tool itself goes fetching for the correct tools and versions for you. ## What is Rokit?

[Rokit][15] is the most popular toolchain manager for Roblox projects. It exists as a direct successor to [Aftman][16], (which itself is a successor to [Foreman][17]). While many projects still use Aftman, [it’s a deprecated tool][18] and it’s generally discouraged to use it in new projects. ## Installing Rokit

Installing Rokit is very easy and requires 1-2 steps. Windows

Grab the latest release from the Rokit [Releases Page][19]. Run the executable you get after unzipping!

MacOS & Linux

Run the following in your terminal:

`curl -sSf https://raw. githubusercontent. com/rojo-rbx/rokit/main/scripts/install. sh | bash
`

Finally, initialize Rokit in your project by running:

`rokit init
`

These steps are directly taken from [the Rokit Repository][20]. # Package Managers

## What are they?

A package manager is a tool that helps you manage libraries and dependencies in your projects. It lets you easily install, update, and remove “Packages” (collections of code, i. e. libraries) that you can use in your Roblox games. ## What is Wally?

[Wally][21] is a package manager for Roblox, similar to `npm` *(which we used earlier!)*. It allows you to easily add third-party libraries to your projects, manage dependencies, and keep everything organized. For example, let’s say you want to use a popular library like react-lua. With Wally, you can simply run a command to install it, and Wally will handle the rest!

## Installing Wally

Installing Wally is very easy!

In your Project folder, run the following command:

`rokit add wally
`

Now, the `wally` command will be available!

## Using Wally

### First steps

Run the following command:

`wally init
`

This command creates Wally’s configuration files for you. Locate the previously mentioned `wally. toml` file
3. Under the `[dependencies]` section, add the following line:
   * `Promise = "evaera/roblox-lua-promise@1. 0. 0"
     `
4. Save the file and run the following command in your terminal:
   * `wally install
     `

This command tells Wally to read the `wally. toml` file, find the new dependency you added, and install it for you. After running this command, you will see Wally has created a new `Packages` folder in your project directory. Inside this folder, you’ll find a folder for the `Promise` package, which contains all the code for that library. 5. To get your packages inside Roblox Studio, run the following command:
   * `azul push --source Packages --destination ReplicatedStorage. Packages --rojo
     `
     * `azul push`: This command is used to “push” specific files & folders *(like this package!)* from your filesystem into Studio. * `--source`: Where the packages are located on your PC. * `--destination`: Where you want the packages to be in Roblox Studio. * `--rojo`: Since Wally packages are structured as Rojo projects, use this flag to tell Azul to treat the source folder as a Rojo project. * *(In place of `--source` or `--destination`, you can also write `-s` and `-d`!)*

> ### [:warning:] Important
>

> -- alongside settings like `--rojo` and `--destructive`. > return {
>   pushMappings = {
>       {
>           source = ". /Packages",
>           destination = { "ReplicatedStorage", "Packages" },
>           destructive = true,
>           rojoMode = true,
>       },
>   }
> }
> `

A few extra notes

Just add them under the `[server-dependencies]` section in `wally. toml` and push them to `ServerScriptService` instead of `ReplicatedStorage`. * In some specific cases, you may notice type errors when using Wally packages. That is because Wally thunks don’t export types. This is a known issue with Wally and can be fixed by running a 3rd-party tool called [`wally-package-types`][26]. # Version Control

## What is Git?

[Git][27] is the most popular version control system in the world. It allows you to keep track of changes to your code, collaborate with others, and manage different versions of your project. ## Why do I need it?

Version control is essential for any project, big or small. It allows you to:
* Keep a history of changes: You can see who made what changes and when. * Easily revert changes: If something breaks, you can easily go back to a previous version of your code. * Collaborate with others: Multiple people can work on the same project without overwriting each other’s work. * Experiment safely: You can create branches to try out new features without affecting the main codebase. While Roblox provides some basic version control features, they are not as powerful or flexible as Git, which is why many developers choose to use Git for their Roblox projects. ## Setting up Git

Visual Studio Code has built-in support for Git, making it super-duper easy to set up and use. Here’s how you can get started:
1. Navigate to the Source Control tab in VS Code (the icon that looks like a branch). 2. Initialize a new Git repository by clicking on the “Initialize Repository” button. * You’ll notice your files now appear green with a “U” next to them, which stands for “Untracked”. This means Git knows about these files, but they haven’t been committed yet!
3. Create a `. gitignore` file in the root of your project to tell Git which files or folders to ignore. * For Roblox projects, you typically want to ignore the `Packages` folder (since it can be reinstalled with Wally) and any local configuration files (like `. vscode/settings. json` or the `wally. lock` file). 4. Stage your changes by clicking the “+” icon next to the files you want to include in your commit. This tells Git that you want to include these changes in your next commit. 5. Write a commit message describing your changes and click “Commit” to save your changes to the local repository. And that’s it! You’ve successfully set up Git for your Roblox project. From here, you can start making commits & creating branches. If you want to distribute your code and collaborate with others, you can also push your local repository to a remote hosting service like GitHub. To do so:
6. Click on the “Publish Branch” button in the Source Control tab. 7. After following the prompts, your code will be pushed to a new repository on GitHub, and you can share the link with your collaborators!
   * For example, take a look at [BetterChassis][28]. A project I published to GitHub using Azul!

### Pack it up!

When publishing your repository, you may want to include more than just your source code. You may want to include assets, UI, or other Roblox instances in order for other developers to easily import your project in the state you left it at. To do this, you can use Azul’s “pack” command, which takes the current state of your game and encodes it into the `sourcemap. json` file. 1. Make sure your game is open in Roblox Studio. 2. Run the following command in your terminal:
   * `azul pack
     `

> ### [:warning:]
> 
> Remember that you’ll need to run this command **every time** you want to “save” the state of your game inside the `sourcemap. json`. It doesn’t update on its own!

If you want to run someone else’s project, you can use 2 commands:
* `azul build --from-sourcemap`:
  * If the project is a full game. * `azul push --from-sourcemap`:
  * If this project is meant to be imported into the game (like a library, a package, or a model). # Wrapping up

And that’s a wrap! You should now have a solid understanding of syncing in Roblox Studio, as well as how to use external tools like Rokit, Wally, and Git to manage your projects more efficiently. If you have any questions, or want any clarifications, be sure to ask below. This is the guide I wish I had back when I first started using external tools for Roblox development, and I hope it helps you on your journey as well!

content.data:
Ótimo, pesquisa completa. Agora vou te explicar tudo de forma clara:

---

## Como funciona um projeto Roblox profissional

### 1. Colaboração em tempo real — Team Create

O Roblox tem uma feature nativa chamada **Team Create**. É o jeito mais simples de trabalhar com seu amigo:

- Você ativa no Studio: **View → Team Create → Enable**
- Convida o usuário Roblox do seu amigo
- Os dois abrem o projeto ao mesmo tempo e editam em tempo real, como Google Docs

**Limitação:** os dois precisam estar online ao mesmo tempo. E se dois mexerem no mesmo script, pode dar conflito.

---

### 2. Workflow profissional com Git — usando Azul

Para trabalhar de forma assíncrona (cada um no seu tempo) e com Git, o fluxo moderno em 2026 é:

```
Roblox Studio ←→ Azul (plugin) ←→ arquivos .lua no PC ←→ Git/GitHub
```

**Como funciona:**
- **Azul** é um plugin do Studio que sincroniza os scripts do jogo com arquivos `.lua` no seu PC em tempo real
- Você edita o script no VS Code → salva → aparece no Studio automaticamente
- Você mexe no Studio → o arquivo `.lua` no PC atualiza
- Aí você faz `git commit` e `git push` normalmente
- Seu amigo faz `git pull` e tem os scripts atualizados

**O que vai para o Git:** só os scripts `.lua`. O cenário (partes, modelos, texturas) fica salvo na nuvem do Roblox.

---

### 3. Como eu (Kiro) posso te ajudar diretamente

Aqui está o que consigo fazer de verdade:

| O que eu faço | Como funciona |
|---|---|
| ✅ **Escrever scripts Lua completos** | Você cola no Studio |
| ✅ **Corrigir bugs nos scripts** | Você me manda o código, eu corrijo |
| ✅ **Planejar toda a arquitetura** | IA dos monstros, sistemas, eventos |
| ✅ **Guardar scripts no GitHub** | Com Azul, os `.lua` ficam no repositório |
| ✅ **Versionar o projeto** | Histórico de tudo que foi feito |
| ❌ **Acessar o Studio diretamente** | Não existe integração direta |
| ❌ **Ver o cenário visualmente** | Só via screenshots que você me manda |

---

### Recomendação para vocês dois

Dado que são iniciantes, sugiro começar simples:

**Fase 1 — agora:** usar **Team Create** para colaborar. Zero configuração, funciona na hora.

**Fase 2 — quando quiserem profissionalizar:** instalar **Azul + VS Code** para ter os scripts no GitHub e eu poder escrever/corrigir código diretamente nos arquivos.

---

Quer que eu monte o passo a passo para ativar o Team Create e convidar seu amigo? É bem rápido.
Pois então, se instalar o Azul, vc pode criar o código madnar para o git e automaticamente vai para o Roblox certo?

Exatamente! O fluxo seria esse:

```
Eu escrevo o .lua → git push → você faz git pull → Azul sincroniza → aparece no Studio
```

Mas tem um detalhe importante: **não é 100% automático**. Você precisaria rodar um comando no PC para puxar as atualizações. O fluxo completo seria:

1. Eu escrevo/corrijo o script aqui
2. Faço `git push` para o GitHub
3. Você abre o terminal e roda `git pull`
4. O Azul detecta a mudança e atualiza o Studio automaticamente

Então você não precisaria nem abrir o código — só rodar `git pull` e ver a mudança aparecer no Studio.

---

**Quer configurar isso agora?**

O que você precisaria instalar no Windows:
- **Node.js** (para rodar o Azul)
- **Azul** (via terminal: `npm install -g azul-sync`)
- **Plugin do Azul** no Roblox Studio (marketplace gratuito)

Leva uns 10 minutos. Quer que eu te guie passo a passo?
Gere um prompt pequeno e efeitvo para eu mandar para o kiro que está no Windows! assim ele já instala tudo

```
Instale o ambiente de desenvolvimento Roblox no Windows seguindo estes passos:
```

--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat save description: Save current conversation to file for later resumption keywords: [chat, save, export, persist, session] related: [chat-load, chat-new] --- # /chat save Save current conversation to file for later resumption. ## Overview The `/chat save` command exports the current conversation state to a JSON file. Conversations are automatically saved to the database per-directory, but this command allows explicit file exports for backup, sharing, or custom storage. ## Usage ``` /chat save <path> /chat save <path> --force ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | File path where conversation will be saved | | `--force`, `-f` | No | Overwrite existing file without prompting | ## How It Works Exports current conversation state as `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON including: - Session metadata (session ID, working directory, timestamps, title) - Complete log entries (message history) File can be loaded later with `/chat load` to restore the conversation. ## Examples ### Example 1: Save to File ``` /chat save my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Saved session to my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Save Without Extension If no extension is provided, `.json` is automatically added: ``` /chat save my-session ``` Saves to `my-session.json`. ### Example 3: Overwrite Existing File ``` /chat save backup.json --force ``` ### Example 4: Save with Path ``` /chat save ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Already Exists **Symptom**: Error "File already exists" **Cause**: File at path already exists and --force not used **Solution**: Use `--force` flag or choose different path ### Issue: Permission Denied **Symptom**: "Failed to write" error **Cause**: No write permission for path **Solution**: Check directory permissions or use different path ### Issue: Invalid Path **Symptom**: Export fails **Cause**: Parent directory doesn't exist **Solution**: Create parent directories first or use existing path ## Related Features - [/chat load](chat-load.md) - Load saved conversation - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start fresh conversation - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI session management ## Technical Details **File Format**: `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON envelope **Structure**: ```json { "format": "kiro-session-export-v1", "metadata": { "session_id": "...", "cwd": "/path/to/dir", "created_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "updated_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", "title": "Session Title", "session_state": "..." }, "log_entries": [...] } ``` **State Preserved**: - Session ID - Working directory (cwd) - Created/updated timestamps - Session title - Log entries (complete message history) **State Not Preserved**: - Tool manager instance (recreated on load) - MCP connections (reconnected on load) - Agent configuration (uses current session's config) **Auto-Extension**: `.json` extension added automatically if no extension provided.

--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: feature title: Session Management description: Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage keywords: [session, save, load, resume, auto-save, storage] related: [chat-save, chat-load, chat] --- # Session Management Automatic session saving, resumption, and file-based storage. ## Overview Kiro CLI automatically saves all chat sessions on every conversation turn. Sessions are stored per-directory as files. Resume from any previous session or export to portable files. ## Auto-Save **Automatic**: Every conversation turn saved **Scope**: Per-directory (each project has own sessions) **Storage**: `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` - `{session_id}.json` - metadata (cwd, timestamps, session state) - `{session_id}.jsonl` - append-only conversation log - `{session_id}.lock` - lock file (exists only when session is active) **Session ID**: UUID for each session ## Managing Sessions ### From Command Line ```bash # Resume most recent session kiro-cli chat --resume # Interactive picker kiro-cli chat --resume-picker # List all sessions kiro-cli chat --list-sessions # Delete session kiro-cli chat --delete-session <SESSION_ID> ``` ### From Chat ```bash # Save to file /chat save <path> # Load from file /chat load <path> ``` **Note**: `.json` extension optional when loading. ## Session Storage **Files**: Sessions auto-saved per-directory to `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Export**: Manual export via `/chat save` **Session ID**: UUID format (e.g., `f2946a26-3735-4b08-8d05-c928010302d5`) ## Examples ### Example 1: Resume Last Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume ``` Continues most recent conversation, restoring the model that was active when the session was saved. ### Example 2: Pick Session ```bash kiro-cli chat --resume-picker ``` Shows list of sessions to choose from. ### Example 3: Export to File ``` /chat save backup.json ``` Exports current session to file. ### Example 4: Save and Load Workflow ```bash # Save current session /chat save ./backup.json # Later, load it back /chat load ./backup.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: No Sessions to Resume **Symptom**: "No saved chat sessions" **Cause**: No sessions in current directory **Solution**: Sessions are per-directory. Navigate to correct directory. ## Related - [/chat save](../slash-commands/chat-save.md) - Save command - [/chat load](../slash-commands/chat-load.md) - Load command - [kiro-cli chat](../commands/chat.md) - CLI options ## Limitations - Sessions stored per-directory - Auto-save to files only (not cloud) - Session IDs are UUIDs (not human-readable) - No cloud sync built-in - No session search by content ## Technical Details **Storage**: File-based in `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/` **Scope**: Sessions keyed by directory path **Auto-Save**: After every conversation turn **Model Preservation**: When resuming via `--resume`, the model active when the session was saved is restored. This includes models switched mid-session with `/model`. The `--model` CLI flag overrides the saved model.

--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-04-09 commit: 4ae084db status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat load description: Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session keywords: [chat, load, import, restore, resume, session, zip, json] related: [chat-save, chat-new] --- # /chat load Load previously saved conversation from file to resume session. ## Overview The `/chat load` command imports a conversation from a saved session file. Supports multiple file formats including the native Kiro export format, zip archives, and legacy V1 sessions. ## Usage ``` /chat load <path> ``` ## Parameters | Parameter | Required | Description | |-----------|----------|-------------| | `path` | Yes | Path to saved session file (.json or .zip) | ## Supported Formats | Format | Description | |--------|-------------| | Kiro | `kiro-session-export-v1` JSON (created by `/chat save`) | | Zip | Archive containing `session_metadata.json` + `conversation_log.jsonl` | | Legacy | V1 `ConversationState` JSON from older CLI versions | | SessionDataOnly | Bare `SessionData` JSON with optional companion `.jsonl` file | ## How It Works 1. Reads the file and auto-detects format 2. Extracts session metadata and conversation log 3. Creates a new session with a fresh UUID 4. Sets `imported_from` field to track origin The loaded session uses your current: - Agent configuration - Model configuration - MCP connections - Hooks ## Examples ### Example 1: Load from JSON File ``` /chat load my-session.json ``` **Output**: ``` Loaded session from /home/user/my-session.json ``` ### Example 2: Load from Zip Archive ``` /chat load backup.zip ``` ### Example 3: Load with Extension Fallback ``` /chat load backup ``` Tries `backup` first, then `backup.zip`, then `backup.json` if not found. ### Example 4: Load from Absolute Path ``` /chat load ~/backups/important-conversation.json ``` ## Troubleshooting ### Issue: File Not Found **Symptom**: "Failed to read" error **Cause**: File doesn't exist at path **Solution**: Check path. Command tries `.zip` then `.json` extensions automatically. ### Issue: Unrecognized Format **Symptom**: "Unrecognized session file format" error **Cause**: File is not a valid session export **Solution**: Ensure file was created with `/chat save` or is a valid legacy export. ### Issue: Invalid Zip Archive **Symptom**: "File has .zip extension but is not a valid zip archive" **Cause**: Corrupted or invalid zip file **Solution**: Re-export the session or use JSON format. ## Related Features - [/chat save](chat-save.md) - Save conversation to file - [/chat new](chat-new.md) - Start a new conversation - [kiro-cli chat --resume](../commands/chat.md) - Resume last conversation ## Technical Details **Extension Handling**: If path has no extension and file not found, tries `.zip` first, then `.json`. **Session ID**: Loaded sessions get a new UUID. The original path is stored in `imported_from`. **Format Detection**: Uses file extension for `.zip`, then probes JSON structure for other formats.

--- doc_meta: validated: 2026-03-22 commit: b8a43f49 status: validated testable_headless: false category: slash_command title: /chat new description: Start a fresh conversation without restarting the CLI keywords: [chat, new, fresh, conversation, reset, start over] related: [chat-load, chat-save, clear] --- # /chat new Start a fresh conversation without restarting the CLI. ## Overview The `/chat new` command creates a brand new conversation session, clearing all messages and starting fresh. The previous session is preserved and can be resumed later with `/chat`. Creates a new ACP session and terminates the old one. ## Usage ### Start a fresh conversation ``` /chat new ``` ### Start with an initial prompt ``` /chat new <prompt> ``` Immediately sends the prompt to the new conversation after creation. ## Examples ### Basic usage ``` > /chat new ✔ New conversation started. Use /chat to return to previous sessions. ``` ### With an initial prompt ``` > /chat new explain how async works in Rust ``` This creates a new conversation and immediately sends "explain how async works in Rust" as the first message. ## How It Works 1. The current session is saved automatically 2. A new session is created with a fresh conversation ID 3. All messages are cleared from the display 4. Model and agent settings are preserved 5. MCP servers remain available (creates new server instances) ## Differences from /clear | Feature | `/chat new` | `/clear` | |---------|-------------|----------| | Creates new session ID | Yes | No | | Previous session resumable via `/chat` | Yes | No | | Clears conversation history | Yes | Yes | | Resets context window | Yes | Yes | | Preserves model selection | Yes | Yes | | Preserves agent selection | Yes | Yes | ## Related - `/chat` — List and load previous sessions - `/chat save` — Save conversation to file - `/chat load` — Load conversation from file - `/clear` — Clear conversation without creating new session ## Limitations - MCP servers are re-initialized in the new session (brief startup delay)
